URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0168169924002138

SUMMARY:
The ensemble Kalman filter algorithm was utilized to assimilate the remote sensing observation data with the WOFOST model , and the results showed that assimilation improved the accuracy of the yield simulation for each treatment.

--- SOURCE 3: Surface Hydrology and Remote Sensing ---
URL: https://weather.ndc.nasa.gov/surface_hydrology/surface_hydrology_kalman_filter.html

SUMMARY:
An extended Kalman filter is applied to nudge the model soil moisture toward the remote measurements, providing an optimal solution for the near-surface soil

FULL CONTENT:
|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | Algorithms Kalman Filter Assimilation Algorithm  Soil moisture information is of vital importance for many applications, most notably agriculture, water resource management and numerical weather prediction. In order to effectively utilize remote sensing data to estimate soil moisture both near the surface and at greater depths, it is necessary to assimilate the data into a modeling system. We have developed a measurement-modeling system incorporating remote microwave observations, a surface hydrology model, a radiative transfer model and Kalman filtering to estimate the three-dimensional soil moisture distribution. In this procedure, the distributed land surface flux-hydrology model SHEELS (Simulator for Hydrology and Energy Exchange at the Land Surface) is used to estimate the vertical and lateral distribution of water. An extended Kalman filter is applied to nudge the model soil moisture toward the remote measurements, providing an optimal solution for the near-surface soil moisture profile. The resulting moisture profiles at high spatial resolution are then used to update the model. The following journal article describes the Kalman filter assimilation system:  [Crosson, W.L., C.A. Laymon, R. Inguva and M. Schamschula, 2002. Assimilating remote sensing data in a surface flux-soil moisture model. *Hydrol. Proc.,* **16**, 1645-1662.](publications/crosson_kalman_2002.pdf)  The modeling system has been applied using spatially-distributed data from the Southern Great Plains ’97 (SGP97) field experiment. In the presence of highly inaccurate rainfall input, using remote microwave data via a Kalman filter update scheme drove the model towards the solution obtained in a ‘control’ simulation which used observed rainfall and was shown to produce soil moisture estimates consistent with in situ observations (Fig. 1). As the update frequency was increased, the resulting soil moisture field more closely resembled the control simulation. The following preprint article describes the application of the assimilation model in the SGP97 experiment and shows the relationship between update frequency and the accuracy of modeled soil moisture:  [Crosson, W., C. Laymon, A. Limaye, W. Khairy, M. Schamschula, T. Coleman and R. Inguva, 2002: Assimilation of remote sensing data in a hydrologic model to improve estimates of spatially distributed soil moisture. Proceedings of IGARSS, *International Geoscience and Remote Sensing Symposium* (June 24-28, Toronto , Canada ), 1168-1170.](publications/crosson_igarss02.pdf)  The results of this research have helped quantify the effectiveness of Kalman filter data assimilation in a particular environment and how the period between assimilation times affects model accuracy. Similar research is needed in other locations and times for in order for us to draw more definitive conclusions. We are currently working on improving the data assimilation algorithm by implementing an ensemble Kalman filter; this will eliminate many of the mathematical instabilities and difficulties of the extended Kalman filter.    Acknowledgements:  This research was supported by NASA’s Terrestrial Hydrology Program through grant no. 622-33-36 to Universities Space Research Association and by NASA grant no. NCCW-0084 to Alabama A&M University, [Center for Hydrology, Soil Climatology and Remote Sensing.](http://www.aamu.edu/hscars/) |

## Algorithms

![](images/title.gif)

Kalman Filter Assimilation Algorithm

Soil moisture information is of vital importance for many applications, most notably agriculture, water resource management and numerical weather prediction. In order to effectively utilize remote sensing data to estimate soil moisture both near the surface and at greater depths, it is necessary to assimilate the data into a modeling system. We have developed a measurement-modeling system incorporating remote microwave observations, a surface hydrology model, a radiative transfer model and Kalman filtering to estimate the three-dimensional soil moisture distribution. In this procedure, the distributed land surface flux-hydrology model SHEELS (Simulator for Hydrology and Energy Exchange at the Land Surface) is used to estimate the vertical and lateral distribution of water. An extended Kalman filter is applied to nudge the model soil moisture toward the remote measurements, providing an optimal solution for the near-surface soil moisture profile. The resulting moisture profiles at high spatial resolution are then used to update the model. The following journal article describes the Kalman filter assimilation system:

[Crosson, W.L., C.A. Laymon, R. Inguva and M. Schamschula, 2002. Assimilating remote sensing data in a surface flux-soil moisture model. *Hydrol. Proc.,* **16**, 1645-1662.](publications/crosson_kalman_2002.pdf)

The modeling system has been applied using spatially-distributed data from the Southern Great Plains ’97 (SGP97) field experiment. In the presence of highly inaccurate rainfall input, using remote microwave data via a Kalman filter update scheme drove the model towards the solution obtained in a ‘control’ simulation which used observed rainfall and was shown to produce soil moisture estimates consistent with in situ observations (Fig. 1). As the update frequency was increased, the resulting soil moisture field more closely resembled the control simulation. The following preprint article describes the application of the assimilation model in the SGP97 experiment and shows the relationship between update frequency and the accuracy of modeled soil moisture:

[Crosson, W., C. Laymon, A. Limaye, W. Khairy, M. Schamschula, T. Coleman and R. Inguva, 2002: Assimilation of remote sensing data in a hydrologic model to improve estimates of spatially distributed soil moisture. Proceedings of IGARSS, *International Geoscience and Remote Sensing Symposium* (June 24-28, Toronto , Canada ), 1168-1170.](publications/crosson_igarss02.pdf)

The results of this research have helped quantify the effectiveness of Kalman filter data assimilation in a particular environment and how the period between assimilation times affects model accuracy. Similar research is needed in other locations and times for in order for us to draw more definitive conclusions. We are currently working on improving the data assimilation algorithm by implementing an ensemble Kalman filter; this will eliminate many of the mathematical instabilities and difficulties of the extended Kalman filter.

![](images/kalman_filter.gif)

![](images/kalman_filter.gif)

Acknowledgements:

This research was supported by NASA’s Terrestrial Hydrology Program through grant no. 622-33-36 to Universities Space Research Association and by NASA grant no. NCCW-0084 to Alabama A&M University, [Center for Hydrology, Soil Climatology and Remote Sensing.](http://www.aamu.edu/hscars/)

The Ensemble Kalman filter: a signal processing perspective  The Ensemble Kalman filter: a signal processing perspective The ensemble Kalman filter (EnKF) is a Monte Carlo-based implementation of the Kalman filter (KF) for extremely high-dimensional, possibly nonlinear, and non-Gaussian state estimation problems. In contrast, the ensemble Kalman filter [8, 3–10162 (1014)."), 9, 1719–1724 (1998).")] (EnKF) was specifically developed as algorithm for high-dimensional _n_. Early results include the Kalman filter (KF) [2, 35–45 (1960).")] as algorithm for linear systems and the Bayesian filtering equations [15.")] as theoretical solution for nonlinear and non-Gaussian systems. 3 A signal processing introduction to the ensemble Kalman filter G Evensen, _Data assimilation: the ensemble Kalman filter_, 2nd ed.

The Ensemble Kalman filter: a signal processing perspective
===========================================================

*   [Michael Roth](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#auth-Michael-Roth-Aff1)[ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0002-4812-346X](https://orcid.org/0000-0002-4812-346X)[1](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#Aff1), 
*   [Gustaf Hendeby](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#auth-Gustaf-Hendeby-Aff1)[1](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#Aff1), 
*   [Carsten Fritsche](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#auth-Carsten-Fritsche-Aff1)[1](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#Aff1)&
*   …
*   [Fredrik Gustafsson](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#auth-Fredrik-Gustafsson-Aff1)[1](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#Aff1)

Show authors
[_EURASIP Journal on Advances in Signal Processing_](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/)**volume 2017**, Article number:56 (2017) [Cite this article](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#citeas)

*   [Metrics details](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x/metrics)

Abstract
--------

The ensemble Kalman filter (EnKF) is a Monte Carlo-based implementation of the Kalman filter (KF) for extremely high-dimensional, possibly nonlinear, and non-Gaussian state estimation problems. Its ability to handle state dimensions in the order of millions has made the EnKF a popular algorithm in different geoscientific disciplines. Despite a similarly vital need for scalable algorithms in signal processing, e.g., to make sense of the ever increasing amount of sensor data, the EnKF is hardly discussed in our field.

This self-contained review is aimed at signal processing researchers and provides all the knowledge to get started with the EnKF. The algorithm is derived in a KF framework, without the often encountered geoscientific terminology. Algorithmic challenges and required extensions of the EnKF are provided, as well as relations to sigma point KF and particle filters. The relevant EnKF literature is summarized in an extensive survey and unique simulation examples, including popular benchmark problems, complement the theory with practical insights. The signal processing perspective highlights new directions of research and facilitates the exchange of potentially beneficial ideas, both for the EnKF and high-dimensional nonlinear and non-Gaussian filtering in general.

1 Introduction
--------------

Numerical weather prediction [[1](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR1 "E Kalnay, Atmospheric modeling, data assimilation and predictability (Cambridge University Press, New York, 2002).")] is an extremely high-dimensional geoscientific state estimation problem. The state _x_ comprises physical quantities (temperature, wind speed, air pressure, etc.) at many spatially distributed grid points, which often yields a state dimension _n_ in the order of millions. Consequently, the Kalman filter (KF) [[2](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR2 "RE Kalman, A new approach to linear filtering and prediction problems. J. Basic Eng. 82(1), 35–45 (1960)."), [3](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR3 "BD Anderson, JB Moore, Optimal filtering (Prentice Hall, Englewood Cliffs, 1979).")] or its nonlinear extensions [[4](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR4 "S Julier, J Uhlmann, H Durrant-Whyte, in Proceedings of the American Control Conference 1995 vol.3.A new approach for filtering nonlinear systems (IEEESeattle, 1995), pp. 1628–1632."), [5](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR5 "M Roth, G Hendeby, F Gustafsson, Nonlinear Kalman filters explained: a tutorial on moment computations and sigma point methods. J Adv. Inf. Fusion. 11(1), 47–70 (2016).")] that require the storage and processing of _n_×_n_ covariance matrices cannot be applied directly. It is well-known that the application of particle filters [[6](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR6 "NJ Gordon, DJ Salmond, AF Smith, Novel approach to nonlinear/non-Gaussian Bayesian state estimation. Radar Signal Process. IEE Proc. F. 140(2), 107–113 (1993)."), [7](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR7 "F Gustafsson, Particle filter theory and practice with positioning applications. IEEE Aerosp. Electron. Syst. Mag. 25(7), 53–82 (2010).")] is not feasible either. In contrast, the ensemble Kalman filter [[8](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR8 "G Evensen, Sequential data assimilation with a nonlinear quasi-geostrophic model using Monte Carlo methods to forecast error statistics. J. Geophys. Res. Oceans. 99(C5), 3–10162 (1014)."), [9](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR9 "G Burgers, JP van Leeuwen, G Evensen, Analysis scheme in the ensemble Kalman filter. Mon. Weather Rev. 126(6), 1719–1724 (1998).")] (EnKF) was specifically developed as algorithm for high-dimensional _n_.

The EnKF

*   is a random-sampling implementation of the KF;

*   reduces the computational complexity of the KF by propagating an ensemble of _N_<_n_ state realizations;

*   can be applied to nonlinear state-space models without the need to compute Jacobian matrices;

*   can be applied to continuous-time as well as discrete-time state transition functions;

*   can be applied to non-Gaussian noise densities;

*   is simple to implement;

*   does not converge to the Bayesian filtering solution for _N_→_∞_ in general;

*   often requires extra measures to work in practice.

Also in the field of stochastic signal processing (SP) and Bayesian state estimation, high-dimensional problems become more and more relevant. Examples include SLAM [[10](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR10 "H Durrant-Whyte, T Bailey, Simultaneous localization and mapping: Part I. IEEE Robot. Autom. Mag. 13(2), 99–110 (2006).")] where _x_ contains an increasing number of landmark positions, or extended target tracking [[11](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR11 "M Baum, UD Hanebeck, Extended object tracking with random hypersurface models. IEEE Trans. Aerosp. Electron. Syst. 50(1), 149–159 (2014)."), [12](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR12 "N Wahlström, E Özkan, Extended target tracking using Gaussian processes. IEEE Trans. Signal Proc. 63(16), 4165–4178 (2015).")] where _x_ can contain many parameters to describe the shape of the target. Furthermore, scalable SP algorithms are required to make sense of the ever increasing amount of data from sensors in everyday devices.

EnKF approaches hardly appear in the relevant SP journals, though. In contrast, vivid theoretical development is documented in geoscientific journals under the umbrella term data assimilation (DA) [[1](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR1 "E Kalnay, Atmospheric modeling, data assimilation and predictability (Cambridge University Press, New York, 2002).")]. Hence, a relevant SP problem is being addressed with only little participation from the SP community. Conversely, much of the DA literature makes little reference to relevant SP contributions. It is our intention to bridge this interesting gap.

There is further overlap that motivates for a closer investigation of the EnKF. First, the basic EnKF [[9](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR9 "G Burgers, JP van Leeuwen, G Evensen, Analysis scheme in the ensemble Kalman filter. Mon. Weather Rev. 126(6), 1719–1724 (1998).")] can be applied to nonlinear and non-Gaussian state-space models because it is entirely sampling based. In fact, the state evolution in geoscientific applications is typically governed by large nonlinear black box prediction models derived from partial differential equations. Furthermore, satellite measurements in weather applications are nonlinearly related to the states [[1](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR1 "E Kalnay, Atmospheric modeling, data assimilation and predictability (Cambridge University Press, New York, 2002).")]. Hence, the EnKF has long been investigated as nonlinear filter. Second, the EnKF literature contains so called localization methods [[13](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR13 "PL Houtekamer, HL Mitchell, Data assimilation using an ensemble Kalman filter technique. Mon. Weather Rev. 126(3), 796–811 (1998)."), [14](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR14 "PL Houtekamer, HL Mitchell, A sequential ensemble Kalman filter for atmospheric data assimilation. Mon. Weather Rev. 129(1), 123–137 (2001).")] to systematically approach high-dimensional problems by only acting on a part of the state vector in each measurement update. These ideas can be directly transferred to sigma point filters [[5](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR5 "M Roth, G Hendeby, F Gustafsson, Nonlinear Kalman filters explained: a tutorial on moment computations and sigma point methods. J Adv. Inf. Fusion. 11(1), 47–70 (2016).")]. Third, the EnKF offers several interesting opportunities to apply SP techniques, e.g., via the application of bootstrap or regularization methods in the EnKF gain computation.

The contributions of this paper aim at making the EnKF more accessible to SP researchers. We provide a concise derivation of the EnKF based on the KF. A literature review highlights important EnKF papers with their respective contributions and facilitates easier access to the extensive and rapidly developing DA literature on the EnKF. Moreover, we put the EnKF in context with popular SP algorithms such as sigma point filters [[4](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR4 "S Julier, J Uhlmann, H Durrant-Whyte, in Proceedings of the American Control Conference 1995 vol.3.A new approach for filtering nonlinear systems (IEEESeattle, 1995), pp. 1628–1632."), [5](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR5 "M Roth, G Hendeby, F Gustafsson, Nonlinear Kalman filters explained: a tutorial on moment computations and sigma point methods. J Adv. Inf. Fusion. 11(1), 47–70 (2016).")] and the particle filter [[6](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR6 "NJ Gordon, DJ Salmond, AF Smith, Novel approach to nonlinear/non-Gaussian Bayesian state estimation. Radar Signal Process. IEE Proc. F. 140(2), 107–113 (1993)."), [7](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR7 "F Gustafsson, Particle filter theory and practice with positioning applications. IEEE Aerosp. Electron. Syst. Mag. 25(7), 53–82 (2010).")]. Our presentation forms a solid basis for further developments and the transfer of beneficial ideas and techniques between the fields of SP and DA.

The structure of the paper is as follows. After an extensive literature review in Section [2](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#Sec2), the EnKF is developed from the KF in Section [3](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#Sec3). Algorithmic properties and challenges of the EnKF and the available approaches to face them are discussed in Sections [4](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#Sec9) and [5](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#Sec13), respectively. Relations to other filtering algorithms are discussed in Section [6](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#Sec19). The theoretical considerations are followed by numerical simulations in Section [7](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#Sec20) and some concluding remarks in Section [8](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#Sec25).

2 Review
--------

The following literature review provides important landmarks for the EnKF novice.

State-space models and the filtering problem have been investigated since the 1960s. Early results include the Kalman filter (KF) [[2](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR2 "RE Kalman, A new approach to linear filtering and prediction problems. J. Basic Eng. 82(1), 35–45 (1960).")] as algorithm for linear systems and the Bayesian filtering equations [[15](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR15 "AH Jazwinski, Stochastic processes and filtering theory (Academic Press, New York, 1970).")] as theoretical solution for nonlinear and non-Gaussian systems. Because the latter approach cannot be implemented in general, approximate filtering algorithms are required. With a leap in computing capacity, the 1990s saw major developments. The sampling-based sigma point Kalman filters [[4](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR4 "S Julier, J Uhlmann, H Durrant-Whyte, in Proceedings of the American Control Conference 1995 vol.3.A new approach for filtering nonlinear systems (IEEESeattle, 1995), pp. 1628–1632."), [5](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR5 "M Roth, G Hendeby, F Gustafsson, Nonlinear Kalman filters explained: a tutorial on moment computations and sigma point methods. J Adv. Inf. Fusion. 11(1), 47–70 (2016).")] started to appear. Furthermore, particle filters [[6](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR6 "NJ Gordon, DJ Salmond, AF Smith, Novel approach to nonlinear/non-Gaussian Bayesian state estimation. Radar Signal Process. IEE Proc. F. 140(2), 107–113 (1993)."), [7](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR7 "F Gustafsson, Particle filter theory and practice with positioning applications. IEEE Aerosp. Electron. Syst. Mag. 25(7), 53–82 (2010).")] were developed to approximately implement the Bayesian filtering equations through sequential importance sampling.

The first EnKF [[8](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR8 "G Evensen, Sequential data assimilation with a nonlinear quasi-geostrophic model using Monte Carlo methods to forecast error statistics. J. Geophys. Res. Oceans. 99(C5), 3–10162 (1014).")] was proposed in a geoscientific journal in 1994 and introduced the idea of propagating ensembles to mimic the KF. A systematic error that resulted in an underestimated uncertainty was later corrected by processing “perturbed measurements.” This randomization is well motivated in [[9](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR9 "G Burgers, JP van Leeuwen, G Evensen, Analysis scheme in the ensemble Kalman filter. Mon. Weather Rev. 126(6), 1719–1724 (1998).")] but also used in [[13](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR13 "PL Houtekamer, HL Mitchell, Data assimilation using an ensemble Kalman filter technique. Mon. Weather Rev. 126(3), 796–811 (1998).")].

Interestingly, [[8](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR8 "G Evensen, Sequential data assimilation with a nonlinear quasi-geostrophic model using Monte Carlo methods to forecast error statistics. J. Geophys. Res. Oceans. 99(C5), 3–10162 (1014).")] remains the most cited EnKF paper 1, followed by the overview article [[16](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR16 "G Evensen, The ensemble Kalman filter: theoretical formulation and practical implementation. Ocean Dyn. 53(4), 343–367 (2003).")] and the monograph [[17](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR17 "G Evensen, Data assimilation: the ensemble Kalman filter, 2nd ed. (Springer, Dordrecht, New York, 2009).")] by the same author. Other insightful overviews from a geoscientific perspective are [[18](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR18 "TM Hamill, in Predictability of Weather and Climate. Ensemble-based atmospheric data assimilation (Cambridge University PressCambridge, 2006)."), [19](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR19 "PL Houtekamer, HL Mitchell, Ensemble Kalman filtering. Q. J. R. Meteorol. Soc. 131(613), 3269–3289 (2005).")]. Many practical aspects of operational EnKF for weather prediction and re-analysis are described in [[19](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR19 "PL Houtekamer, HL Mitchell, Ensemble Kalman filtering. Q. J. R. Meteorol. Soc. 131(613), 3269–3289 (2005).")–[21](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR21 "GP Compo, JS Whitaker, PD Sardeshmukh, N Matsui, RJ Allan, X Yin, BE Gleason, RS Vose, G Rutledge, P Bessemoulin, S Brönnimann, M Brunet, RI Crouthamel, AN Grant, PY Groisman, PD Jones, MC Kruk, AC Kruger, GJ Marshall, M Maugeri, HY Mok, O Nordli, TF Ross, RM Trigo, XL Wang, SD Woodruff, SJ Worley, The twentieth century reanalysis project. Q. J. R. Meteorol. Soc. 137(654), 1–28 (2011).")]. Whereas the aforementioned papers were mostly published in geoscientific outlets, a special issue of the IEEE Control Systems Magazine appeared with review articles [[22](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR22 "S Lakshmivarahan, D Stensrud, Ensemble Kalman filter. IEEE Control. Syst. 29(3), 34–46 (2009).")–[24](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR24 "G Evensen, The ensemble Kalman filter for combined state and parameter estimation. IEEE Control. Syst. 29(3), 83–104 (2009).")] and an EnKF case study [[25](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR25 "J Mandel, J Beezley, J Coen, M Kim, Data assimilation for wildland fires. IEEE Control. Syst. 29(3), 47–65 (2009).")]. Still, the above material was written by EnKF researchers with a geoscientific focus and in the application-specific terminology. Furthermore, references to the recent SP literature and other nonlinear KF variants [[5](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR5 "M Roth, G Hendeby, F Gustafsson, Nonlinear Kalman filters explained: a tutorial on moment computations and sigma point methods. J Adv. Inf. Fusion. 11(1), 47–70 (2016).")] are scarce.

Some attention has been devoted to the EnKF also beyond the geosciences. Convergence properties for _N_→_∞_ have been established using different theoretical analyses of the EnKF [[26](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR26 "R Furrer, T Bengtsson, Estimation of high-dimensional prior and posterior covariance matrices in Kalman filter variants. J. Multivar. Anal. 98(2), 227–255 (2007).")–[28](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR28 "J Mandel, L Cobb, JD Beezley, On the convergence of the ensemble Kalman filter. Appl. Math. 56(6), 533–541 (2011).")]. Statistical perspectives are provided in the thesis [[29](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR29 "M Frei, Ensemble Kalman Filtering and Generalizations (Dissertation, ETH, Zürich, 2013). nr. 21266.")] and the review [[30](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR30 "M Katzfuss, JR Stroud, CK Wikle, Understanding the ensemble Kalman filter. Am. Stat. 70(4), 350–357 (2016).")]. A recommended SP view that connects the EnKF with Bayesian filtering and particle methods, including convergence results for nonlinear systems, is [[31](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR31 "F Le Gland, V Monbet, V Tran, in The Oxford Handbook of Nonlinear Filtering, ed. by D Crisan, B Rozovskii. Large sample asymptotics for the ensemble Kalman filter (Oxford University PressOxford, 2011), pp. 598–634.")]. Examples of the EnKF as tool for tomographic imaging and target tracking are described in [[32](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR32 "M Butala, R Frazin, Y Chen, F Kamalabadi, Tomographic imaging of dynamic objects with the ensemble Kalman filter. IEEE Trans. Image Process. 18(7), 1573–1587 (2009).")] and [[33](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR33 "J Dunik, O Straka, M Simandl, E Blasch, Random-point-based filters: analysis and comparison in target tracking. IEEE Trans. Aerosp. Electron. Syst. 51(2), 1403–1421 (2015).")], respectively. Brief introductory papers that connect the EnKF with more established SP algorithms include [[34](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR34 "S Gillijns, O Mendoza, J Chandrasekar, B De Moor, D Bernstein, A Ridley, in American Control Conference, 2006. What is the ensemble Kalman filter and how well does it work? (IEEEMinneapolis, 2006), pp. 4448–4453.")] and [[35](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR35 "M Roth, C Fritsche, G Hendeby, F Gustafsson, in European Signal Processing Conference 2015 (EUSIPCO 2015). The ensemble Kalman filter and its relations to other nonlinear filters (IEEEFrance, 2015).")]. The latter also served as basis for this article.

The majority of EnKF advances are still documented in geoscientific publications. Notable contributions include deterministic EnKF that avoid the randomization of [[9](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR9 "G Burgers, JP van Leeuwen, G Evensen, Analysis scheme in the ensemble Kalman filter. Mon. Weather Rev. 126(6), 1719–1724 (1998).")] and propagate an ensemble of deviations from the ensemble mean [[16](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR16 "G Evensen, The ensemble Kalman filter: theoretical formulation and practical implementation. Ocean Dyn. 53(4), 343–367 (2003)."), [36](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR36 "JL Anderson, An ensemble adjustment Kalman filter for data assimilation. Mon. Weather Rev. 129(12), 2884–2903 (2001).")–[38](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR38 "JS Whitaker, TM Hamill, Ensemble data assimilation without perturbed observations. Mon. Weather Rev. 130(7), 1913–1924 (2002).")]. Their common basis as square root EnKF and the relation to square root KF [[3](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR3 "BD Anderson, JB Moore, Optimal filtering (Prentice Hall, Englewood Cliffs, 1979).")] is discussed in [[39](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR39 "MK Tippett, JL Anderson, CH Bishop, TM Hamill, JS Whitaker, Ensemble square root filters. Mon. Weather Rev. 131(7), 1485–1490 (2003).")]. The computational advantages in high-dimensional EnKF with small ensembles (_N_≪_n_) come at the price of adverse effects, including the risk of filter divergence. The often encountered underestimation of uncertainty can be counteracted with covariance inflation [[40](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR40 "JL Anderson, SL Anderson, A Monte Carlo implementation of the nonlinear filtering problem to produce ensemble assimilations and forecasts. Mon. Weather Rev. 127(12), 2741–2758 (1999).")]. A scheme with two EnKF in parallel that provide each other with gain matrices to reduce unwanted “inbreeding” has been suggested in [[13](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR13 "PL Houtekamer, HL Mitchell, Data assimilation using an ensemble Kalman filter technique. Mon. Weather Rev. 126(3), 796–811 (1998).")]. The benefit of such a double EnKF is, however, debated [[38](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR38 "JS Whitaker, TM Hamill, Ensemble data assimilation without perturbed observations. Mon. Weather Rev. 130(7), 1913–1924 (2002)."), [41](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR41 "PJ van Leeuwen, Comment on “data assimilation using an ensemble Kalman filter technique”. Mon. Weather Rev. 127(6), 1374–1377 (1999).")]. The low-rank approximation of covariance matrices can yield spurious correlations between supposedly uncorrelated state components and measurements. Localization techniques such as local measurement updates [[13](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR13 "PL Houtekamer, HL Mitchell, Data assimilation using an ensemble Kalman filter technique. Mon. Weather Rev. 126(3), 796–811 (1998)."), [16](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR16 "G Evensen, The ensemble Kalman filter: theoretical formulation and practical implementation. Ocean Dyn. 53(4), 343–367 (2003)."), [42](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR42 "E Ott, BR Hunt, I Szunyogh, AV Zimin, EJ Kostelich, M Corazza, E Kalnay, DJ Patil, JA Yorke, A local ensemble Kalman filter for atmospheric data assimilation. Tellus A. 56(5), 415–428 (2004).")] or covariance tapering [[14](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR14 "PL Houtekamer, HL Mitchell, A sequential ensemble Kalman filter for atmospheric data assimilation. Mon. Weather Rev. 129(1), 123–137 (2001)."), [43](https://asp-eurasipjournals.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s13634-017-0492-x#ref-CR43 "TM Hamill, JS Whitaker, C Snyder, Distance-dependent filtering of background error covariance estimates in an ensemble Kalman filter. Mon. Weather Rev. 129(11), 2776–2790 (2001).")] let the measurement only affect a part of the state vector. In other words, localization effectively reduces the dimension of each measurement update. Inflation and localization are essentia

enriched_points.expanded_content: The Ensemble Kalman Filter (EnKF) enhances yield forecasts by assimilating remote sensing data into models, improving prediction accuracy significantly, as shown in various studies.
Real-time updates in EnKF facilitate continuous integration of new remote sensing data, allowing for dynamic adjustments in yield predictions, thus enhancing agricultural decision-making.
